item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This one is kind of a mess. The tone is all over the place, the use of English is funky all through the skip and there's a whole lot of stuff that just doesn't congeal well. I ended up just reading as fast as I could because I wanted it to end and it just kept going on and on. You could (and should) trim it hard so it becomes a little more focused and interesting.

As a sidenote, it befuddles me how you managed to write so much about this Taylor guy without making any kind of sense. I just don't understand what you are trying to say or the underlying story of the stuff, it reads like incoherent mumbo-jumbo. Try retaining the core aspects of the story you want to tell while cutting out all the superfluous bits. It would make for a more rewarding experience overall.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really wanted to like this. The anomaly itself was very well done and written well, and the initial hooks of the story got me really curious! However, it doesn't feel like any of the setup paid off. I think being a little clearer with Taylor's story would do this piece a lot of good. It's a really good start, after all!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Major tone issue. It has got a neat idea, but was executed poorly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
… first it says half the team was gone. Then, a third.
Shouldn't the second say that they only had a third left?
Or is he trying to say they lost a third of that remaining half?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Silly technical detail: you don't need a license plate to ID the owner of a car. The VIN can be read from a plate on the dash. So one of the following would make more sense:

1. The VIN has been obliterated with a tool of some kind, or

2. The VIN was checked and the car was found to be the researcher's

item.title: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Edit: approved by author in chat

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

The humanoid entities in this skip remind me of that of SCP-1312. They both involve possibly mindless humanoid entities trying to protect/contain something, whether it's a site or a person.

Editing this:

it is not currently known if instances are capable of understanding said speech.

To this:

it is not currently known if instances are capable of understanding said speech1.

It's also come to my attention that BananaRepublic has deleted their Wikidot account. Did they give their skips away to anybody?

Footnotes
1. This behavior is similar to that of the entities within SCP-1312. A link between the two anomalies has yet to be established.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What's this sposed to mean "Damn good shots, they were. And good marksmen, as well."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We kind of need more info on why this Taylor guy is what he is. No person goes from being in a highly paid job as a researcher to eating humans and wild animals alone with only just some family and domestic troubles.

item.title: Questions
item.content:encoded:
I like the whole "insta-box" thing and the idea that someone would use it to lock themselves away.
What I did not get was the WHY of the story. There were the letters, calls and emails, but all I got was that this guy did something what made his family angry. Pissin off the folks is bad, but not enough to lock oneself away for ever and ever. So, what do? What did this guy do that made him want to lock him self away?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Miscellaneous
- Hide

Project Planet cell

Should 'cell' not also be capitalized?

higher ups

Feel like a dash would make this look less janky.

Dr. Henderson: This sounds like it was top secret.

This observation seems nonsensical. There's lots of Labs projects that could feasibly be considered black operations. Surely they have an understanding of this? 'Top secret' could easily be replaced by 'it must've been exciting,' and the conversation wouldn't miss a beat.

Higher ups

This is the second time you've used 'higher-ups to denote a group of individuals in the apex of command. It feels repetitive. Perhaps you could replace it with 'PR' or something like that? That way, it'd be more clear who the company was denying themselves to.

corpses. Just, Jesus.The

Spacing.

I liked this. I think you manage to capture the sense of an experiment going wrong rather well; the pacing is good, the characters are decent enough, in that their motivations make sense and their dialogue is feasible, and the Foundation's response to the situation seems appropriate. Good job all around.

item.title: OK. Not upvotable.
item.content:encoded:
Couple minor things:

+ show block
– hide block

applied to a currently living organism

I'd cut 'currently', it reads like you're saying the organisms have to be alive right now.

now known as SCP-2891-1

What were they known as before? This was immersion-breaking. I'd change this to something else.

although the average number

The average number of land? Just say 'average area'.

the designation of objects

for, not of

SCP-2891-2 is the designation of objects and structures formed by SCP-2891 around the affected area of SCP-2891-1.

I'd end this at 'formed by SCP-2891'.

found to manifest at varying times in the stages of SCP-2891

But…in the stages, they only appear in one stage.

in order to appear as said officers

For the second time, I'd cut this. It makes the sentence redundant and speculative. Why attribute motive to something that may be wholly mindless?

barbed wire increased,

Again, this makes grammatical sense in the context of the list. Also, sweet jesus the run-on sentence.

Nitpicks aside.

While this has seen more revisions, it's still not where it could be. The interview, while no longer full of outdated PL tropes, is still bland straight exposition that gets in the way of the character. Having this neat, sound little backstory of the anomaly presented with a little bow on top sucks out the mystery, and detracts from the potential emotional impact of the character's story.

The second log from the MTF is simply cheap stock gore, that again, detracts from the final portion. I'll add that it also takes away from the imagery for me. You could instead reinforce the picture painted by the (stages) log, and immerse readers in the discovery of Taylor and their tragic situation…

Why you'd take this from terrifying confinement- playing on autophobic and claustophobic fears- and end up at more 'blood and guts' is beyond me. You had a good thing without that.

I think more focus should be on Dr. Taylor and the correspondences. I'd open that up some and add some dates to them to help cement the forward progression. Good luck.

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded: Sorry for this. I will keep this in mind in the future. I truly apolgize for not sourcing it.

item.title: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded:
You failed to source the image on this page, but since it's not localized I just went to where it was hosted on wikimedia commons. You have to list your image source on the discussion page.

Here it is, just so it's listed. It's public domain.

https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Mexican_Army.jpg

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one is definitely a lot better! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Me gusta. This version is a tick better.

+ Things I like:
– hide block

-Living organisms only. Avoids tons of plot holes.
-Prom Labs not being a complete screwup. A lot more believeable, a lot less cliche, and leaves me with quite a few questions.
-Added some limits and dimensions, leaving reasonable ambiguity as well.
-I do like the squick, but there appears to be little reason for it, see below.

+ Things I don't like:
– hide block

-Kirk still seems like the least professional researcher in existence (hyperbole, of course). Even under an interview, one would expect a Foundation researcher to be more formal.
-Containers: how big? Are they my Tupperware containers? Or toxic waste barrels?
-Second paragraph of the description has some redundancy in regards to the stage section.
-The -3's seem to squick for no reason, at least not any aforementioned one. Mention the gun's on the wall before you shoot it, y'know? Why do they weirdly slaughter stuff? Murdering an object to death is enough to defend -1, right?

+ Things I feel neutral about:
– hide block

-The touchy-feely doesn't really get me. It feels kinda shoehorned in. That being said, it adds another aspect to think about to this article.

A weird thing to note: You went from effect oriented to squick to touchy-feely all in one fell scip. Huh. Don't try doing to much in one scip, to me this one has less direct impact, but I suppose more overall.

All in all, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the Kentucky state

Probably should be "State of Kentucky"; I'm guessing you aren't from around here…

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Ok, second times the charm, right? this is a rewrite of my own rewrite of an article made by Dr Dice. Hopefully, its much better than the perviouse version.

EDIT: Thank you to cyan, AidenEldritch, Zyn, Bluemorpho, Westydude, Hampo2003, DrBleep, and so many many others for looking over this. Sorry if i forget your name, i'm terrible at remembering them.

+ Original
- Hide whatever

Item #: SCP-2895

Object Class: Euclid

Special Containment Procedures: All removed SCP-2895-1 instances must be contained in Standard Secure Chests in Site-17. Any areas that have been infected with SCP-2895 must be demolished if removal of SCP-2895-1 instances has no effect. Any electronic instances of SCP-2895-1 are to be deleted as a standard employee procedure.

Description: SCP-2895 is an anomalous pathogen that effects most of the western hemisphere and parts of Europe. SCP-2895's primary anomalous effect is the ability to materialize any sort of security system (hereby designated as SCP-2895-1 instances) that are created using the pathogen as a "building block" to materialize these structures.

SCP-2895's security systems grow more advanced and more secure overtime, as untreated buildings have developed security cameras, automated turrets, electronic keypads, barbed wire, mines, and, on rare occasions, saw traps. SCP-2895's effects are not limited to physical security however, as many agents have reported them being locked out of their personal computers, and security measures increased. Any other attempts to access an infected computer have led to numerous deaths via a security memetic generated by SCP-2895.

Discovery Log: SCP-2895 was found on ██-██-████ in an abandoned shack in Royal Oak, Maryland after many teenage bodies were found near the perimeter with gunshot wounds and dismembered limbs. The bodies were taken in for autopsy, and the bullets were shown to be .303 caliber rounds shot at 2,000 rounds per minute, roughly the rate of fire of a standard military-grade minigun. At the time, it was unknown how the shack had gotten hold of a military grade weapon, until similar cases were reported in Raleigh, North Carolina; Hamburg, Germany; and Los Angeles, California, giving it SCP status.

Addendum/2895-A: After researching the effects that SCP-2895 has on buildings, a table of stages have been provided to recognize symptoms of SCP-2895
[[collapsible show="+ Infection Stages" hide="- Hide"]]

Stage # Time Since Infection Description Threat Level
Stage 1 5-10 minutes Padlocks, "Get out!" signs and picket fences materialized. No major changes, curable Safe
Stage 2 3-10 hours More resistant padlocks, more durable fences, barbed wire, and bulletproof glass materialized. Moderate changes, curable Unsafe
Stage 3 2-3 days Electronic locking systems, metal fences, undeveloped turrets, security cameras and more barbed wire materialized. Major changes, treatable, not curable Unsafe
Stage 4 1 week Titanium alloy fences, more developed turrets, bear traps, 180 degree security cameras, reinforced steel house material are materialized. Major changes, incurable, doors removed Very Unsafe
Stage 5 2-3 weeks Concrete fences, mines, military-grade turrets, 360 degree security cameras, built-in tasers, barbed wire increased, saw traps, and watchtowers materialized. Major changes, incurable, windows removed Dangerous

Unlike building stages, electronic security only has 3 stages of infection, as shown below

Stage # Time Since Infection Description Threat Level
Stage 1 3-12 minutes Passwords changed, antivirus security updated, and minor websites blocked. Minor changes, curable Safe
Stage 2 40-60 minutes Passwords re-generated, certain files not allowed on system, social media blocked. Minor changes, curable Unsafe
Stage 3 4-5 hours Passwords generated by RNG, no files, disks, or flash drives allowed on system, internet blocked, security memetics generated. Major changes, incurable Dangerous

[[collapsible]]

Addendum/2895-B: Instances of SCP-2895 were also recovered on a raid on an abandoned Prometheus Labs facility on ██-██-████ with the following note with broken test tubes of a black liquid.

Prometheus Labs, Inc. Product Pamphlet

Hello there survivalists, sufferers of xenophobia, and people who are just generally afraid. We here at Prometheus Labs, Inc. have made the product just for you! The simply dubbed "Fortifier Goo" will easily make a suburban home into Fort Knox in no time! Just let this stuff spread around your house and watch the magic happen! This is no trick, it's all science; with the genetically engineered molecules used to create padlocks, barbed wire and more!

Item Price: $250,000 U.S., $200,000 Canada, $1,717,825 China, $234

+ First Rewrite
- Hide whatever

Item # SCP-2891

Object Class: Safe

Special Containment Procedures: All containers of SCP-2891 are to be kept on Site-43 in High Priority Containment Wing-C. SCP-2891 may only be removed with the permission of two Level 4 researchers, and only for the purposes of testing. All SCP-2891-1, -2, and -3 instances are to be destroyed following testing, with their remains kept in Wing-B.

Description: SCP-2891 is an anomalous liquid that can cause an environment to undergo significant changes when applied to an organism or object through direct contact (SCP-2891-1). When applied to SCP-2891, it can cause the manifestation of structures and objects designed to defend SCP-2891-1. These structures, now known as SCP-2891-2, usually take the form of security cameras, turrets, and containment cells. It is currently unknown how these manifestations are formed.

Over time, SCP-2891-2 instances begin to become more and more complex. Untreated SCP-2891-2 instances following completion of stage 5 are found to be equipped with security cameras, automated turrets,1 electronic keypads, barbed wire, land mines, steel doors, and tear gas.

Additionally, humanoid entities are found to manifest at varying times in the stages of SCP-2891. These humanoid entities, designated SCP-2891-3, are all genetically identical to each other. SCP-2891-3 instances often have identical uniforms and use similar weaponry as local enforcement officers in order to appear as said officers. It should be noted that while instances of SCP-2891-3 are capable of speech in the language predominate in the affected area, it is not currently known if instances are capable of understanding said speech.

[[collapsible show="Stages" hide="Access Granted"]]

Stage 1: The initial stage of SCP-2891-1. Padlocks, reinforced fencing around the affected area, and barred windows manifest. Stage usually lasts for three to five hours after initial application.

Stage 2: SCP-2891-1's clothing begins to alternate from their original designs, often including or removing materials in order to make the removal of said clothing more difficult for the particular instance of SCP-2891-1.

Stage 3: At stage 3, SCP-2891-1's clothing binds to SCP-2891-1's skin. Electronic locking systems, half-formed turrets, security cameras and barbed wire materialize around the affected area.

Stage 4: SCP-2891-1 is bound to a specific location within the affected area. This is usually accomplished with belts, metal bars, full-body strait jackets, and ropes. While this occurs, SCP-2891-1 begins to undergo a form of psychological passivity, allowing for its capture with little to no resistance. The affected area is equipped with titanium alloy fences, more developed turrets, tear gas, 180-degree security cameras, and reinforced steel doors.

Stage 5: SCP-2891-1 enters into a vegetative state, only surviving based on the sustenance it receives from SCP-2891-3 instances, which, along with concrete walls, land minds, military-grade turrets, 360-degree security cameras, built-in tasers, barbed wire increased, and saw traps, also appear.

Stage 6: The final stage of SCP-2891. This stage begins only after the death of SCP-2891-1.2 When this occurs, SCP-2891-2 instances begin to dematerialize from the affected area, returning the affected area to its pre-SCP-2891 form. All instances of SCP-2891-3 die over the course of three days.3

[[/collapsible]

Currently, there are over thirteen containers filled with SCP-2891 in Foundation control.

Addendum-2891-1

SCP-2891 was first discovered following the absorption of the many subsidiaries and divisions of GoI-2312,4 specifically for Prometheus Defense and Biotech, under a project known as "Project Plant Cell". It was also discovered that a Dr. Kirk, a Foundation researcher and former Prometheus Labs employee, had worked on the project.

Interviewed: Dr. Kirk
Interviewer: Dr. Henderson
Date: 06/11/2003

<Begin Log>

Dr. Henderson: Good evening, Dr. Kirk.

Dr. Kirk: Evening.

Dr. Henderson: Would you be willing to answer some questions regarding SCP-2891?

Dr. Kirk: Sure.

Dr. Henderson: Thank you. Now-

[Paper shuffling]

Dr. Henderson: -what was your involvement with the Prometheus Labs project known as "Project Planet cell"?

Dr. Kirk: [Sighs] What do you wanna know?

Dr. Henderson: What was the purpose of the project?

Dr. Kirk: It was meant to be many things, but the primary point of it was meant to be a semi-containment producer. You would slather the object or person you needed to contain and presto! You got your own containment cell in no time flat.

Dr. Henderson: And what were you planning on doing with this object?

Dr. Kirk: Selling it to the Foundation, of course. You see, the Foundation had come to the higher ups, asking for help. They needed something that could temporarily manifest locations that could contain an object or creature that needed to be recovered quickly and at a moment's notice.

Dr. Henderson: When was this?

Dr. Kirk: I'd say… the early '60s? I forget. Just know that it was at around that time that I was sent into the Appalachians to a newly dug-in Site for the production of a new project, and I was one of the lead researchers.

Dr. Henderson: This sounds like it was top secret.

Dr. Kirk: Oh, it was. Higher ups denied any involvement in the project, of course. Needed to keep up appearances, we do have standards.

[Dr. Kirk laughs]

Dr. Kirk: Progress was good. Now, sure, there were some kinks in the actual goo. The actual cells would produce more than was needed. A simple cell would develop into a full fucking prison, the guards were inherently violent and trigger happy, the things would get into your clothes, etc. etc. But those were just normal parts of the job, nothing unusual.

[Dr. Henderson does not respond]

Dr. Kirk: That was until one day, one of my co-workers, Jared, comes into work, a fucking mess. You could tell that the fucker had the flu. I would have sent him home if he was in my department, but, he wasn't, and so I had to have a snotty man in my lab.

[Dr. Henderson does not respond]

Dr. Kirk: Well, during one of the test trials, Jared, while slathering the subject down, gets it on him. It's not that hard to miss, the thing was made orange for a reason, and so he was put in isolation. I should have known that something was wrong when one of my guards were complaining about something orange coming out of Jared's nose. Thought the guy was just seeing things, I just didn't know…

[Dr. Henderson does not respond]

Dr. Kirk: Well, one night, I decide to go check up on Jared, see how things are going. Well, I get up and take my keycard, and go out to see how he's doing. Purely scientific interest, of course. I get near the guard area, and I see there dark figures near the entrance. They seem to be… dangling from, like, the ceiling or something.

[Dr. Henderson does not respond]

Dr. Kirk: I open the door, and the first thing that hits me is the putrid smell. It smelled like a dead body or something. I think 'Did something die in here' and try looking around. I then see small creatures dangling from the ceiling and hanging by a… by a hook. I look around for a light-switch, find one, and see that…

Dr. Henderson: See what?

Dr. Kirk: I see that hundreds of men, all wrapped in straitjackets, gagged and bound. Hundreds of identical men, all walking around the room with guns, all in lock step. They're all silent, and turn their heads to me. I… can you promise me something?

Dr. Henderson: What is it?

Dr. Kirk: Don't let any former Labs employees hear this.

Dr. Henderson: I will try, but I can't make any promises.

Dr. Kirk: I ran. I ran and ran and ran and ran until I got to the main security office in Area-22. I told them about the 'outbreak' and how we needed to stop it. Half an hour later, I see that an army has been raised of officers and maintenance workers. At least 200 to 300 men and women, armed to the teeth.

Dr. Henderson: What was the cause of this 'outbreak'?

Dr. Kirk: Jared's flu. Apparently, the goo must have gotten into his system and manifested itself within his flu-ness. Footage from his cell showed orange goop coming out of his nose. I looked over the cameras during the fighting. Clones clashing against men and women. They fought with a sort of savagery that I've never seen before. When they ran out of ammo, they would bash their skulls in with the butt of their guns and charge our men. They would fall on top of grenades in order to prevent the others from being harmed, and just… hell.

Dr. Henderson: How did you escape?

Dr. Kirk: I remember yelling to my comrades below, telling them about how I was going to ask for reinforcements from the surface. How there was a local Labs militia near the mountains in case of something like this. I remember looking at their faces as I sealed the damn entrances, and how they banged against the door as the guards wiped them out.

[Dr. Kirk pauses]

Dr. Henderson: What happened to the facility?

Dr. Kirk: Buried. All of it, just… buried underneath a hundred feet of concrete, and all evidence wiped from the record. I can still hear their screams at night, sometimes.

<End Log>

An investigation into this location is ongoing.

Footnotes
1. equipped with military-grade ammunition
2. This varies between individual instances of SCP-2891-1, some surviving only for three months, while other instances surviving for up to three years.
3. Cause of death is currently unknown.
4. Also known as Prometheus Labs Inc.
